intro:
Wisdom Literature is the generic designation for the OT books that address the art of living well within God's created moral order: Job, Proverbs, Ecclesiastes, some Psalms (notably 1, 37, 49, 73, 104, 119, 128), and the Song of Songs. The Apocrypha adds Sirach (Ecclesiasticus) and the Wisdom of Solomon. Wisdom literature is characterized by its attention to patterns in human experience, its employment of literary forms (proverbs, riddles, instructions, disputation speeches, numerical sayings, acrostics), and its concern with practical ethics, the problem of suffering, the limits of human knowledge, and the nature of true happiness (shalom).

Unlike the Law and the Prophets, wisdom literature rarely appeals to the Sinai covenant, the Exodus, or Israel's specific history. It addresses human experience in universal terms — family, work, speech, wealth, friendship, death — and presents its insights as derivable from careful observation of God's created order as well as divine revelation. This gives the genre an ecumenical quality: Proverbs 22:17-24:22 shows extensive parallels with the Egyptian 'Instruction of Amenemope,' and Job's protagonists are non-Israelite. The tradition assumes that God's created moral order is accessible to all humans made in his image, and that observing it leads to flourishing. The NT wisdom tradition is visible in the Epistle of James, the parables of Jesus (which employ the sapiential method of observation and insight), and in Paul's explicit identification of Christ as 'the wisdom of God' (1 Cor. 1:24, 30) — the one in whom 'are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge' (Col. 2:3).
